    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "I recall back in my day, when I was learning plumbing as a child in Naples, we had to be very careful about the measurements. You couldn't just take someone's word for it; you had to verify everything yourself because one wrong turn could mean disaster. Now with social media, there are all these fake stories floating around like hot oil on a sunny day - they're everywhere! I'm not an expert in this digital stuff, but from my life experiences, the key is always verification and skepticism. When I was young, my father who had his master's degree would say 'Let me see that again' before relying on it completely. Same thing here. Don't believe everything you hear online unless you can fact-check it yourself or consult reliable sources.\n\nSince I'm not really active on social media myself (my time is spent fixing pipes, not scrolling), I rely on others to handle this properly. But from what I've heard about the platforms and their algorithms, they need to do more too. They should implement better checks before content gets posted publicly, just like we plumbers check our work three times before it's done.\n\nIt also reminds me of my own creative side - sometimes people post fake things because they're trying to be clever or funny, but you have to draw the line somewhere when misinformation could cause harm. I think if everyone approaches social media with a bit more caution and creativity in thinking critically about what they share, we can manage this better.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I recall back in my day, when I was learning plumbing as a child in Naples, we had to be very careful about the measurements. You couldn't just take someone's word for it; you had to verify everything yourself because one wrong turn could mean disaster. Now with social media, there are all these fake stories floating around like hot oil on a sunny day - they're everywhere! I'm not an expert in this digital stuff, but from my life experiences, the key is always verification and skepticism. When I was young, my father who had his master's degree would say 'Let me see that again' before relying on it completely. Same thing here. Don't believe everything you hear online unless you can fact-check it yourself or consult reliable sources.

Since I'm not really active on social media myself (my time is spent fixing pipes, not scrolling), I rely on others to handle this properly. But from what I've heard about the platforms and their algorithms, they need to do more too. They should implement better checks before content gets posted publicly, just like we plumbers check our work three times before it's done.

It also reminds me of my own creative side - sometimes people post fake things because they're trying to be clever or funny, but you have to draw the line somewhere when misinformation could cause harm. I think if everyone approaches social media with a bit more caution and creativity in thinking critically about what they share, we can manage this better.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1. Draw analogy from my past experience as a plumber needing precise measurements.
2. Emphasize the importance of verification due to lack of direct exposure but understanding through others' stories.
3. Suggest involving reliable sources similar to consulting one's knowledgeable father.
4. Recommend multiple checks and critical thinking, mirroring my high extraversion and agreeableness in wanting collaboration without being overly trusting.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective with a touch of wisdom from life experience
